segments.s207-g17: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' introducing what he is free of. دستور و گنجور are two coordinated nouns ('dastur' here = order/command/regulation; 'ganjūr' is a noun 'treasury' rather than an adjective), joined by و. The phrase then repeats the preposition as از before تاج و تخت for rhythmic parallelism; تاج و تخت is a conventional paired phrase ('crown and throne').
segments.s207-g18: Again ز introduces another pair of abstractions: کمی و بیشی ('kemi' = scarcity/shortage; 'bishi' = surplus/excess), with و linking them. After that, از introduces a final pair ناز و بخت, where ناز here is poetic for vanity/pride and بخت means fate/destiny. The repeated prepositions and paired nouns form a rhythmic list of things he is independent of.
segments.s207-g19: همه is the subject ('all' / 'everyone/everything'), followed by the predicate بی‌نیازست ('bi-niyāz-st' = 'is without need' / 'self-sufficient'). Note the use of the third-person singular copula -ست with a collective subject, a feature common in classical/poetic Persian. The clause then adds a contrastive coordinate: و ما بنده‌ایم ('and we are servants') where بنده‌ایم is first-person plural present of بودن with بنده as predicate noun 'servants'.
segments.s207-g20: به introduces an instrumental/causal prepositional phrase here ('by/at/under'), so به فرمان و رایش means 'by/at his command and counsel/judgment' (رایش = his judgment/opinion). This prepositional phrase modifies سرافگنده‌ایم, an adjectival/predicative form 'we are abashed/abased' in the first-person plural, giving the reason or circumstance of our shame.
segments.s207-g21: This is a coordinated noun phrase + verb clause: the items 'شب و روز و گردان سپهر' are listed as objects, with the verb 'آفرید' (past tense) at the end, typical Persian SOV order. 'گردان سپهر' is a genitive phrase where 'گردان' (lit. 'revolving') qualifies 'سپهر' (heavens/sky).
segments.s207-g22: A second coordinated list with the same verb 'آفرید' understood for the whole line; the items 'خور و خواب و تندی و مهر' are enumerated and then closed by the past verb. Each noun stands alone (no overt conjunction other than 'و').
segments.s207-g23: 'جز او را مدان' is an archaic/poetic admonition: 'جز ... مدان' = 'acknowledge/know no one except ...'. Here 'را' is the object/topic marker attaching emphasis to 'او'. 'مدان' is an archaic form (see note) used imperatively or jussive in poetry; it governs the restriction introduced by 'جز'. 'کردگار بلند' is a noun phrase in apposition specifying 'او' (the High/lofty Creator).
segments.s207-g24: Two short clauses joined: 'کز او شادمانی' = 'from him (comes) joy' where 'کز' = 'from/that from whom' introduces the source; 'و ز او مستمند' = 'and (the) needy are from him / depend on him' where 'ز' (poetic variant of 'از') marks source or dependence and the copula ('است') is omitted but understood. Word order places the source preposition before the pronoun for emphasis.
segments.s207-g9: This is a poetic, somewhat inverted clause: 'از او' (from him) preposes the source, followed by the verbal phrase 'گشت پیدا' (گشت = became, پیدا = apparent/visible). The logical subject of the verb is 'مکان و زمان' (place and time), so the construction reads 'place and time became apparent from him.' گشت is a past/preterite verb used in an archaic/poetic register.
segments.s207-g10: 'پی مور' is a genitive-like unit meaning 'the track/footprint of the ant' (پی used as 'track'). The rest 'بر هستی او نشان' uses the preposition بر (upon) and places the noun نشان (sign/trace) after it; the verb is omitted (elliptical), so the sense is 'an ant's trail bore a sign upon his being' or 'left a mark on his existence.' The implied verb is classical/poetic omission rather than a new tense.
segments.s207-g11: A range introduced by the poetic preposition 'ز' (from) + 'گردنده خورشید' (the revolving/turning sun). 'تا' here marks the other end of the range ('as far as'/'to') and attaches to 'تیره خاک' (dark earth). Word order is nominal: the two ends of the span surround the connector 'تا'.
segments.s207-g12: This is an elliptic list of elements following the previous range: 'دگر' (other/again) qualifies the items 'باد و آتش' (wind and fire). 'همان' modifies 'آب پاک' to mean 'that same pure water' (i.e. likewise the pure water). There is no explicit verb; the clause continues the cataloging begun in the previous segment.
segments.s207-g13: 'به هستی یزدان' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to the existence of God' (i.e. concerning God's being). The verb 'گواهی دهند' (they give testimony / they bear witness) is present-plural and generalizing — a common poetic present used to state that things testify to God. Word order places the object/target of the testimony before the verb.
segments.s207-g14: Here 'روان' (soul) carries the second-person enclitic 'ت' (your) and the object marker 'را', so 'روان‌ترا' = 'your soul' as the direct object. 'آشنایی دهند' means 'they give familiarity / make acquainted' (آشنایی دادن). Again the plural verb is generalizing; the implied subject is the same as the previous segment.
segments.s207-g15: 'ز هرچ آفریدست' is a classical relative-like phrase: 'of whatever has been created' (here 'آفریدست' is a poetic past/passive form meaning 'was created' or 'has been created'). The main predication 'او بی‌نیاز' follows: 'he is self-sufficient / needs nothing.' The order places the domain ('of all that was created') before the statement about God.
segments.s207-g16: 'تو' (you) is the subject, 'در پادشاهیش' is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in his kingship/sovereignty' (پادشاهی + possessive -ش), and 'گردن‌فراز' is a predicate adjective meaning 'proud' or 'lofty.' The clause states your proud/lofty stance within his rule; the simple present is implied in this descriptive poetic line.
segments.s207-g1: به نام is a fixed opening phrase meaning “in the name (of)”; note the preposition به takes the meaning “by/in” here. خداوند خورشید و ماه is a verbless poetic noun phrase where خداوند governs خورشید و ماه (the ezāfe is omitted for metrical reasons): “the Lord of the Sun and the Moon.”
segments.s207-g2: که is a relative pronoun “who/which/that.” دل را is the definite object marked by را. به نامش is an agentive/means phrase “by/through his name,” where -ش is the enclitic 3rd-person possessive (“his”). خرد داد راه is poetically inverted word order: خرد (wisdom) + داد (gave) + راه (the way/path) = “gave the way to wisdom” (i.e., “gave the heart the path to wisdom”).
segments.s207-g3: This is a nominal phrase in apposition: خداوند (the Lord/God) followed by two complements joined by و. هستی and راستی are nouns in parallel (“being/existence” and “truth/uprightness”), with هم adding the sense “also/indeed” before راستی.
segments.s207-g4: نخواهد is the negative 3rd-person present/future of خواستن here (he will not / does not wish / will not allow). ز تو is the poetic preposition “from/with respect to you” (z → from/of you). کژی و کاستی are the two nouns joined by و: “crookedness/wrongness and deficiency/shortcoming.” The clause thus says the Lord will not (permit/find) crookedness or deficiency in you.
segments.s207-g5: This is a poetic list attached to خداوند: three divine/celestial names are coordinated by و. Each name functions as a title or attribute of the Lord (elliptical copula). The sequence evokes cosmic forces (planets/epithets) rather than forming an independent verb.
segments.s207-g6: از اویم is a compact construction: از + او + ـیم (poetic first‑person form) meaning “I have from him / I am of him.” نوید is an abstract noun “tidings/promise.” و connects the second clause; بدویم is a poetic vocalic form meaning “I run/hasten to him” (بد + او + یم structure), and امید is the object “hope.” The line joins possession of a promise with turning to him in hope.
segments.s207-g7: ستودن is the infinitive “to praise.” مر is an archaic object/clitic meaning “me” (here idiomatic: “for me”/“to me”), while او را is the object “him” with the canonical را marker. ندانم is the 1st-person present negative of دانستن “I do not know.” همی is an archaic particle marking ongoing/habitual aspect; overall: “I do not know how to praise him” (i.e., praise exceeds my capacity).
segments.s207-g8: از اندیشه is an ablative phrase “from the thought/of thinking (about him).” جان is “soul/life,” the object of برفشانم, which is a 1st‑person verb meaning “I scatter/shed/let fall” (poetic, transitive). همی again marks ongoing or emphatic aspect. The clause reads as a hyperbolic poetic image: “from thinking (of him) I scatter my soul.”
segments.s207-g25: 'شگفتی به گیتی ز رستم بس است' has the predicate 'بس است' (is enough) at the end, a frequent poetic order. 'شگفتی' (marvels/wonders) is the subject, 'به گیتی' (= 'in the world' / 'for the world') is a locative/dative adverbial, and 'ز رستم' marks the source/agent 'because of Rostam'.
segments.s207-g26: 'کزو داستان بر دل هرکس است' is a relative/result clause: 'کزو' = 'from whom' referring back to Rostam, 'داستان' = 'tale/story' is the subject, and 'بر دل هرکس است' = 'is upon the heart of every person' (literally 'on the heart of each one is'). Note the copula 'است' placed at the end as usual.
segments.s207-g27: 'سر مایه مردی و جنگ' is a compound noun phrase meaning 'the chief source/capital of manliness and of war'. 'از اوست' ('از' + pronoun + poetic 'ست' for 'است') is the predicate meaning 'is from him' or 'comes from him'. Note the poetic contraction 'ست' for 'است'.
segments.s207-g28: A parallel coordinate clause: 'خردمندی و دانش و سنگ از اوست' lists three nouns as qualities/sources and closes with 'از اوست' meaning 'are from him'. In poetic diction 'سنگ' (literally 'stone') functions here metaphorically as firmness/solidity, though the lexical sense is 'stone/rock'.
segments.s207-g29: This is a parallel prepositional simile: به خشکی چو پیل = 'on/at the land, like an elephant'. Here به is the preposition marking location 'to/towards/at' (used poetically as 'on/in'), خشکی is 'the dry place / land', چو introduces the simile 'like', and پیل is the simile's noun 'elephant'. The second half repeats the same pattern with و joining two coordinated prepositional phrases (به دریا نهنگ = 'and in the sea, a whale'). There is no copula; the phrases are descriptive images linked by و.
segments.s207-g30: A string of epithets describing the same subject: خردمند (an adjective 'wise'), و linking the items, بینادل (a rare/uncertain word left untranslated in many editions), و linking again, and مرد سنگ which is a noun + postmodifier 'man (مرد) of stone (سنگ)'. The order is adjective(s) and noun-phrase; the final item (مرد سنگ) functions as a substantive epithet ('stone-man' = resolute/unyielding) following the preceding adjectives.
segments.s207-g31: An initial adverbial phrase plus verb phrase: کنون 'now' sets the time; رزم کاموس is a noun phrase 'the battle of Kāmūs' (a proper-name genitive). پیش آوریم is a verbal compound meaning 'we bring forward / present' — آوریم is first-person plural present/subjunctive form ('we bring / we shall bring' or 'let us bring'), and پیش here indicates 'forward / before (the audience)'.
segments.s207-g32: A source-and-goal phrase: ز دفتر 'from the book' (ز = poetic variant of از), به گفتار خویش آوریم 'we bring into our speech'. به marks the direction/goal ('into / into the phrasing of'), گفتار is 'speech / account', and خویش appears here split into خوی + ش for metrical reasons: خوی (root) plus the enclitic ش (possessive pronoun) forming 'خویش' = 'one's own / our own'. آوریم repeats the same 1st-person-plural verb 'we bring / we relate' (here with the sense 'relate/report from the book').

sentences.s207-s9: He needs none of law or treasury, nor of crown and throne, nor of scarcity and surplus, nor of pride and fate.
sentences.s207-s10: All are without need, and we are servants; at his command and judgment we are abashed.
sentences.s207-s11: He created night and day and the revolving heavens; he created the sun, sleep, swiftness, and love.
sentences.s207-s12: Acknowledge none but Him, the High Creator; from Him comes joy, and the needy are dependent on Him.
sentences.s207-s5: From him place and time became apparent; even an ant's track left a mark upon his being.
sentences.s207-s6: From the revolving sun to the dark earth, and again the wind and the fire, and that same pure water.
sentences.s207-s7: They bear witness to God's existence, and they give acquaintance to your soul.
sentences.s207-s8: Of everything that has been created he is self-sufficient; you are proud in his kingship.
sentences.s207-s1: In the name of the Lord of the Sun and the Moon, who by His name gave the heart the path to wisdom.
sentences.s207-s2: The Lord of being and of truth will not bring (allow) crookedness or defect to you.
sentences.s207-s3: The Lord of Bahram and Keyvān and Shīd — from him I have tidings, and toward him I run with hope.
sentences.s207-s4: I do not know how to praise him; from the thought of him I scatter my soul.
sentences.s207-s13: Rostam's marvels are enough for the world; his tale is in the heart of every man.
sentences.s207-s14: The source of manliness and of war is him; wisdom and learning and firmness (stone) are from him.
sentences.s207-s15: On the land like an elephant, and in the sea like a whale — wise, bīn‑adel, and a man of stone.
sentences.s207-s16: Now we bring forward the battle of Kāmūs; from the book we bring it into our speech.
